timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Jeremiah at the Potter's

sections.header: Verses 1-9 — "Go and Buy a Clay Jar"

heb.paragraph: God commands Jeremiah to buy a potter's clay jar (baqbuq, v.1) and take it to the Valley of Ben Hinnom. The baqbuq is an onomatopoeia — the word imitates the gurgling sound of liquid pouring from a narrow-necked flask. Unlike the soft clay on the wheel (ch. 18), this fired vessel cannot be reshaped. When broken, it is irreparable — the shift from soft clay to hardened pot signals that the time for reshaping has passed.

refs.note: The Topheth oracle from the Temple Sermon — child sacrifice at the Valley of Ben Hinnom. Chapter 19 returns to the same site for the sign-act.
Christ "will rule them with an iron scepter; he will dash them to pieces like pottery" — echoing the imagery of smashed vessels as divine judgment.

notes.note: The delegation includes elders of the people and elders of the priests — representatives of both civil and religious leadership. They are witnesses to their own sentencing.
The crimes cataloged: filling the place with blood of the innocent, building high places to Baal, burning children in fire as offerings. Each charge escalates the severity of the indictment.

The judgment: God will "make them fall by the sword" and give their carcasses as food for birds. The cannibalism prophecy (v.9) — parents eating children during the siege — was literally fulfilled in 586 BC (Lam 4:10).

Calvin notes the progression from chapter 18: "First God showed the soft clay that could be reformed. Now he shows the hardened vessel that can only be destroyed. The time for reformation has passed.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The baqbuq is a narrow-necked jug, typically about 10 inches tall, used for storing and pouring liquids. Once fired, it cannot be reworked — only shattered.
The "Potsherd Gate" (šaʿar haḥarsît) led to the Hinnom Valley — appropriately named, since broken pottery was discarded in this area.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom reads chapters 18-19 as a deliberate pair: soft clay (reformable) → fired flask (irreformable). The theological development mirrors the historical development: the window for repentance has closed. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann identifies the broken flask as "the end of negotiation." The potter's wheel allowed dialogue between potter and clay. The shattered flask permits no response — the verdict is enacted, not debated. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Chapter 19 escalates the potter imagery from chapter 18. The soft clay on the wheel could be reshaped; this fired flask can only be smashed. The setting — the Valley of Ben Hinnom (Topheth) — connects this sign-act to the child sacrifice indictment of 7:30-34. Jeremiah returns to the most horrific location in Jerusalem's landscape to perform the most violent prophetic gesture yet: shattering the flask as an irreversible declaration of judgment.

sections.header: Verses 10-15 — "I Will Shatter This Nation and This City"

heb.paragraph: Jeremiah shatters (šābar) the flask in front of the witnesses (v.10). The verb šābar is used for violent, irreparable breaking — shattering bones (Ps 51:8), breaking yokes (Jer 28:10), demolishing structures. A shattered clay vessel cannot be mended. The irreversibility is the message.

refs.note: "You will break them with a rod of iron; you will dash them to pieces like pottery" — royal psalm imagery applied to divine judgment.
The direct consequence: Pashhur the priest beats Jeremiah and puts him in stocks — the temple establishment responds to the sign-act with violence.

notes.note: "I will smash this nation and this city just as this potter's jar is smashed and cannot be repaired." The explicit interpretation leaves no ambiguity — Topheth itself will become a cemetery because there is no room to bury the dead elsewhere.

Jeremiah returns to the temple court and announces the same judgment to the full assembly. The move from Topheth to temple connects the two locations — both are sites of false worship that will be judged.

Calvin emphasizes the visual power of the act: "The sound of shattering clay would ring in the ears of the witnesses long after the words were forgotten. God accommodates his message to human senses.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase "cannot be repaired" (lōʾ-yûkal lĕhērāpēʾ) uses the root rāpāʾ ("to heal") — the vessel cannot be "healed." The same root describes the prophets'  superficial healing in 6:14 and 8:11. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom notes that the move from Topheth to temple connects the book's two great judgment sites. The Valley of Slaughter and the house of God are equally condemned — sacred space offers no immunity from judgment. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann observes that Jeremiah's return to the temple court is an act of extraordinary courage — he goes back to the institution that has already tried to silence him. The prophet cannot be domesticated. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: The smashing of the flask is performed publicly before the elders, and then Jeremiah returns to the temple court to deliver the same message to the full population (v.14). The sign-act at Topheth and the verbal proclamation at the temple form a two-stage announcement: visual demonstration followed by verbal interpretation. This public defiance will provoke the first physical attack on Jeremiah (ch. 20).

themes.note: Judgment peaks at 10 — the smashed flask is the book's most extreme symbol of irreversible divine sentencing. Mercy at 1: the time for mercy has passed.

rows.label: vv.1-3
rows.text: Buy the Flask: Setting the Stage

rows.label: vv.4-9
rows.text: The Charges: Blood, Baal, and Child Sacrifice

lit.note: The chapter escalates ch. 18's potter imagery from reformable clay to irreparable flask. The smashing is the central act — visual, public, irrevocable.

hebtext.note: God commands Jeremiah to buy a potter's clay jar (baqbuq, v.1) and take it to the Valley of Ben Hinnom. The baqbuq is an onomatopoeia — the word imitates the gurgling sound of liquid pouring from a narrow-necked flask. Unlike the soft clay on the wheel (ch. 18), this fired vessel cannot be reshaped. When broken, it is irreparable — the shift from soft clay to hardened pot signals that the time for reshaping has passed.

Jeremiah shatters (šābar) the flask in front of the witnesses (v.10). The verb šābar is used for violent, irreparable breaking — shattering bones (Ps 51:8), breaking yokes (Jer 28:10), demolishing structures. A shattered clay vessel cannot be mended. The irreversibility is the message.

ppl.name: Jeremiah
ppl.text: Jeremiah returns to the most horrific location in Jerusalem's landscape to perform the most violent prophetic gesture yet: shattering the flask as an irreversible declaration of judgment…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Broken Flask

translations.text: I will make them eat the flesh of their sons and daughters, and they will eat one another’s flesh because their enemies will press the siege so hard against them to destroy them.’

And I will make them eat the flesh of their sons and their daughters, and everyone shall eat the flesh of his neighbor in the siege and in the distress, with which their enemies and those who seek their life afflict them.’

“ ‘In this place I will ruin the plans of Judah and Jerusalem. I will make them fall by the sword before their enemies, at the hands of those who want to kill them, and I will give their carcasses as food to the birds and the wild animals.

And in this place I will make void the plans of Judah and Jerusalem, and will cause their people to fall by the sword before their enemies, and by the hand of those who seek their life. I will give their dead bodies for food to the birds of the air and to the beasts of the earth.

thread.text: The Topheth oracle from the Temple Sermon — child sacrifice at the Valley of Ben Hinnom. Chapter 19 returns to the same site for the sign-act.
Christ "will rule them with an iron scepter; he will dash them to pieces like pottery" — echoing the imagery of smashed vessels as divine judgment.
"You will break them with a rod of iron; you will dash them to pieces like pottery" — royal psalm imagery applied to divine judgment.
The direct consequence: Pashhur the priest beats Jeremiah and puts him in stocks — the temple establishment responds to the sign-act with violence.

coaching.tip: The clay jar oracle complements the potter's house (ch. 18). In chapter 18, the clay is reworked — there's hope. In chapter 19, the jar is shattered — 'it cannot be repaired' (v. 11). Same material, opposite outcome. Together they represent God's two options: reformation or destruction. Jeremiah has moved from the possibility of chapter 18 to the finality of chapter 19.

Topheth in the Valley of Ben Hinnom — where children were sacrificed to Molek — gives us the word Gehenna, which Jesus uses for hell (Mark 9:47). Jeremiah's oracle against this valley (vv. 6–9) establishes it as the geographic symbol of judgment. The worst place in Israel's moral geography becomes the New Testament's image for ultimate consequence. Theology grows in specific soil.
